skill.name: code-refactoring
skill.description: Simplify and refactor code while preserving behavior, improving clarity, and reducing complexity. Use when simplifying complex code, removing duplication, or applying design patterns. Handles Extract Method, DRY principle, SOLID principles, behavior validation, and refactoring patterns.
skill.summary: Simplify and refactor code while preserving behavior, improving clarity, and reducing complexity
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 48 potential issues including external_commands, network, filesystem, and cryptographic patterns. Manual review confirms all findings are false positives. The skill is documentation-only (SKILL.md contains code examples for teaching refactoring techniques). No actual shell execution, network requests, filesystem operations, or cryptographic code exists in this skill. Risk level: SAFE.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution Detection
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 36 instances of 'external_commands' at various SKILL.md lines. These are FALSE POSITIVES - the skill contains TypeScript code examples using template literals (backticks) for teaching refactoring patterns. No actual shell execution occurs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Template literals in TypeScript examples use backticks for string interpolation - this is documentation content, not shell commands. No actual execution occurs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URLs Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 3 URLs in SKILL.md. These are FALSE POSITIVES - they are reference links to external books and resources in the documentation section (lines 471-473), not network requests made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are reference links to refactoring.com, oreilly.com, and wikipedia.org in the References section - standard documentation, not executable network calls.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Sequence Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 3 path traversal sequences. These are FALSE POSITIVES - the paths '..//code-review/SKILL.md' and '..//backend/testing/SKILL.md' are relative links to related skills in the documentation (lines 483-484).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Paths are documentation cross-references to related skills, not filesystem operations performed by the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 6 instances of weak cryptographic algorithms. These are FALSE POSITIVES - no cryptographic code or algorithms exist in this skill. The detections appear to be misidentifications of other patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms no cryptographic algorithms exist in this skill. Lines 3 and 5 contain YAML frontmatter ('name', 'tags'), and line 324 contains a markdown checklist item - none involve cryptography.

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers simplify complex code while preserving behavior. It provides step-by-step refactoring patterns like Extract Method, DRY principle, and SOLID design to improve code clarity and reduce technical debt.
actual_capabilities: Apply Extract Method pattern to break long functions into smaller, focused units
Remove code duplication using DRY (Do Not Repeat Yourself) principle
Replace complex conditionals with polymorphism for better extensibility
Group related parameters into objects to reduce function signatures
Apply SOLID principles for better object-oriented design
Validate behavior preservation through testing before and after refactoring
limitations: Does not automatically refactor code - provides guidance and patterns only
Does not execute tests or verify behavior - user must run their own test suite
Does not handle database schema migrations or data transformation
Does not provide real-time complexity analysis of large codebases

use_cases.title: Simplify Long Functions
use_cases.description: A developer has a 200-line function that does too much. Use Extract Method to break it into smaller, focused functions with clear names.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers working with legacy code

use_cases.title: Remove Duplicated Logic
use_cases.description: A codebase has the same validation logic repeated in multiple places. Apply DRY principle to extract common logic into reusable functions.

use_cases.title: Improve Object-Oriented Design
use_cases.description: A class has multiple responsibilities causing tight coupling. Apply SOLID principles to separate concerns into well-defined classes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Refactoring Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this function to be more maintainable. Apply the Extract Method pattern to break it into smaller, focused functions:
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a long function that does multiple things

prompt_templates.title: Remove Duplication
prompt_templates.prompt: Find and remove duplicated code in this codebase. Apply the DRY principle by extracting common logic into reusable functions:
prompt_templates.scenario: When you notice repeated code patterns

prompt_templates.title: Apply SOLID Principles
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this class and suggest how to apply SOLID principles. Identify which principles are violated and how to fix them:
prompt_templates.scenario: When working with complex object-oriented code

prompt_templates.title: Behavior-Preserving Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to refactor this code. First, help me understand its current behavior (inputs, outputs, side effects). Then propose refactoring changes that preserve behavior, and tell me how to validate the changes:
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to refactor without changing functionality

output_examples.input: Refactor this function: function processOrder(order) { /* 100 lines of mixed validation, calculation, inventory check, and order creation */ }
output_examples.output:
## Refactoring Plan

### Original Function: processOrder (100 lines)

### Changes Made:
1. **Extracted validateOrder()** - Handles input validation
2. **Extracted calculateTotal()** - Computes order total with tax and shipping
3. **Extracted checkInventory()** - Validates stock availability
4. **Extracted createOrder()** - Creates the order in database

### New Structure:
```typescript
async function processOrder(order: Order) {
  validateOrder(order);
  const total = calculateTotal(order);
  await checkInventory(order);
  return await createOrder(order, total);
}
```

### Behavior Preserved:
- [x] Same validation rules
- [x] Same total calculation
- [x] Same inventory checking
- [x] Same order creation

output_examples.input: These two functions have duplicated logic: getActiveUsers() and getActivePremiumUsers()
This class violates Single Responsibility. How should I refactor it?
output_examples.output:
## SOLID Analysis

### Original: User class with 3 responsibilities
- Data storage (save to DB)
- Email sending
- Report generation

### Refactored Structure:

| Class | Responsibility |
|-------|----------------|
| User | Data container |
| UserRepository | Database operations |
| EmailService | Communication |
| UserReportGenerator | Reporting |

### Benefits:
- Each class has one reason to change
- Easier to test in isolation
- Flexible to swap implementations
- Better collaboration in teams

best_practices: Write tests before refactoring to establish behavior baseline
Make small, incremental changes - one refactoring at a time
Run tests after each change to verify behavior is preserved
Use clear, descriptive names for extracted functions and variables
Commit after each successful refactoring for easy rollback
anti_patterns: Refactoring and adding new features simultaneously - separates concerns properly
Skipping tests because 'the change is simple' - always verify behavior
Creating abstraction layers that add complexity without clear benefit
Refactoring code you do not understand - analyze behavior first
Ignoring performance implications of abstraction - profile when needed

faq.question: What is Extract Method refactoring?
faq.answer: Extract Method is a technique where you take a long function and break it into smaller, focused functions. Each new function has a clear name that describes what it does. This improves readability and makes code easier to test and maintain.

faq.question: How do I refactor without breaking functionality?
faq.answer: Always write or run tests before refactoring to establish a baseline. Make small, incremental changes. Run tests after each change. Verify that inputs produce the same outputs before and after refactoring.

faq.question: When should I apply SOLID principles?
faq.answer: Apply SOLID principles when you notice code that is difficult to change, test, or extend. Look for classes that have too many responsibilities, code that needs modification for multiple reasons, or logic that is hard to test in isolation.

faq.question: What is the DRY principle?
faq.answer: DRY stands for Do Not Repeat Yourself. It means avoiding duplicated logic in your codebase. When you find repeated code, extract the common logic into a single reusable function or module.

faq.question: How do I know if my refactoring is complete?
faq.answer: Refactoring is complete when: all tests pass, the code is easier to understand, each function has a single responsibility, there is no duplication, and the code follows consistent patterns.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically refactor my code?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides guidance, patterns, and examples for manual refactoring. It helps you understand what to do and how to do it safely, but you must apply the changes yourself and verify with tests.
